I'd prefer to continue using absolute imports. PEP8 recommends them for most cases:

> Absolute imports are recommended, as they are usually more readable and tend to be better behaved (or at least give better error messages) if the import system is incorrectly configured (such as when a directory inside a package ends up on sys.path)

> However, explicit relative imports are an acceptable alternative to absolute imports, especially when dealing with complex package layouts where using absolute imports would be unnecessarily verbose

The weakref callback is called with the weakref as argument, when the referent is no longer available. From the docs:
> If callback is provided and not None, and the returned weakref object is still alive, the callback will be called when the object is about to be finalized; the weak reference object will be passed as the only parameter to the callback; the referent will no longer be available.

That's the reason I switched back to `WeakValueDict` and use `weakref.finalize`.

This is an unnecessary backwards incompatibility, since custom networks might not have `request_response`.

Instead, I would propose that this line be:

`network_response=network_response`

This has the benefits of:
- Is backwards compatible.
- Works with custom subclasses of `NetworkResponse`.
- Still allows the user to access `network_response.request_response`, if `network_response` is an instance of `DefaultNetworkResponse`.
- Not tying us to the `requests` library even further.
- Probably never again needing to add more fields to the exception, since we now include a maximal amount of information.

We could also avoid adding `access_token_used`, since the user could use `exception.network_response.access_token_used` instead. It's a minor difference, we could do either. Having it directly on the object is slightly more clear and direct and less work for the user to access, but is another property that we would have to maintain forever for backwards compatibility.

Looking again, the idea behind the comment was that if you passed `dists` rather than `dists.rjb` into the call to `_compute_mean` function in the DrouetAlpes2015Rjb, and also passed the `dists` object to the `_compute_term2` function and only inside the `_compute_term2` function indicate:
```python
def _compute_term2(self, C, mag, dists):
    """
    This computes the term f2 equation 8 Drouet & Cotton (2015)
    """
    return (C['c4'] + C['c5'] * mag) * np.log(np.sqrt(dists.rjb **2 + C['c6'] **2)) + C['c7'] * dists.rjb
```
Then in the subclasses you would only need to modify the `_compute_term2` function (and the `REQUIRES_DISTANCES`) in the subclasses and not need to repeat so much of the code of the `get_mean_and_stddevs` method. 
That said however, this is a cosmetic change to reduce the number of lines of code and doesn't really have any impact, so it is not critical.
Note, though, that changing the `REQUIRES_DISTANCES` attribute to reflect the distance metric used in each subclass is critical!

The name `groupByN` doesn't feel right to me. It makes it sound like it's grouping by N. Lots of people reading this name will think the function takes in an "n" parameter... maybe they'll think it's essentially a [partition](https://clojuredocs.org/clojure.core/partition) function.

It's identical to `itertools.groupby` when given one sequence (arity 2), and it just supports other arities (4, 6, ...). I think `groupby2` would be a good name. The more we piggyback on people's knowledge about `itertools.groupby`, the better. The goal is to make this idea of a "groupby" seem as simple as it actually is.

I have been thinking more about the points of abstraction here, and I do think that it should be broken up or abstracted, so that the `annotate` method isn't a single long block of code, because there are meaningfully different processes that occur, which might be useful to separate out.

One thought I had was to separate out the different structures that we look for into different functions or methods. For instance, right now this looks for two main formulations:

- The first is for lemmas which correspond to infections, deaths, or hospitalizations in the noun chunk, e.g. "3 cases".
- The second is to look for person lemmas in the noun chunk, and then look for verbs in a certain position in the dependency tree to indicate that they were infected or hospitalized, e.g. "a man was sickened".

Breaking these out is useful because:

1. It means that it's easier to see what bit of code generated an item, especially if I create a `metadata[token_attributes]` metadata block.
2. It gives a clear path to expansion. Adding new capabilities, e.g. new structures for infection events, or adding the capability to look for things like "suspected" etc., should go in new methods (or functions, or wherever these should go), and added to the list of methods called by `InfectionAnnotator.annotate()`

However, I'm not certain the right… break-point, if that makes sense? This code currently exists in `InfectionSpan.init()` because I kept moving it around, and this is where it was when I finished.

- Methods of `InfectionAnnotator` that are called by `annotate`. This means they're more constrained in their application, which is fine. They would take as their argument the `AnnoDoc` which is being annotated, so that they could do things like use the entire spaCy noun chunks tier as their input — and this might be useful because this means that we can look for different parts of speech as the "seeds" for annotations, depending on the method.
- Functions defined inside `infection_annotator.py`, like some of the lower-level functions which currently generate counts.
- This was also why I at one point wanted to have a `MetaGen()` class which generated metadata for a token.

In one formulation, then, `InfectionAnnotator()` might look roughly like (pseudocode):

```python
def InfectionAnnotator():
    def init(self, doc):
        self.doc = doc

    def annotate(self):
        spans = []
        spans.append(self.noun_chunks_infection_lemmas(self.doc))
        spans.append(self.noun_chunks_with_person_lemmas_and_infection_verbs(self.doc))
        spans.append(self.other_as_yet_undefined_methods(self.doc))
        doc.tiers['infections'] = AnnoTier(spans)

    def self.noun_chunks_infection_lemmas(doc):
        definition
        return(spans)

    def self.noun_chunks_with_person_lemmas_and_infection_verbs(doc):
        definition
        return(spans)

    def self.other_as_yet_undefined_methods(doc):
        definition
        return(spans)
```

Another way would see those last three methods as functions. I don't think it's worth putting them in as their own annotator subclasses.

Rather than using an unconditional `.then`, with a `match` in it:
```
.then(|res| match res {
  Ok(_) => ...,
  Err(e) => ...,
)
```

it may be a little cleaner to use `map`/`map_err`. Because our success case may fail (that's what the `try_future!` is doing - returning a failure case if `grpc_result` is an error), we need to use `and_then` rather than `map`:

```
Delay::new(...)
  .map_err(|err| err.to_string())
  .and_then(|_| future::ok(future::Loop::Continue((try_future!(grpc_result), iter_num + 1))).to_boxed() as BoxFuture<_, _>)
```

Then, noting that `Future::and_then` accepts something which returns an `IntoFuture`, we can actually just return a `Result`, and not need to worry about all of the `Future`-specific nonsense of `try_future!`, `to_boxed()` and so on:

```
Delay::new(...)
  .map_err(|err| err.to_string())
  .and_then(|_| Ok(future::Loop::Continue((grpc_result?, iter_num + 1))))
```

If you run the tests from this branch (or look at the printed output on travis, probably), you can see the action sequences we're trying to produce.  We want to give action ids for the actions in the target sequence, where the ids are positions in the list of all actions.  But there's an action that is `<r,d> -> ['lambda x', d]`.  The trouble is that `<r,d>` isn't in the type declaration; it comes from `<d,r> -> [<<#1,#2>,<#2,#1>>, <r,d>]`.  So how do I get that rule into the list of actions?

A couple of options:

1. Keep track of the actual type object when creating the `valid_actions` dict, and just add a `lambda` to every key that's a complex type (or maybe every key?).  We need to keep track of the type object because recovering the input and output types from the type string is a pain.
2. Have a mapping from production rule string to something like `LAMBDA` and `VAR` for the lambda and var rules, and have those not depend on the type of the left hand side.

Any other options?  I looked briefly through the data, and it looked like the only place that lambdas showed up was right after `reverse` - it could be that we could hard code something here that would simplify things a bit.

Rather than storing these at the module level, I would recommend storing the `sharding_config` as a property of the `VMC` instance.  Populate it in the `__init__` method and then pull values from it from whatever property you set it to:

```python
def sample(self, shard_id, gas=None):
    if gas is None:
        gas = self.config['DEFAULT_GAS']
    ...
```

Reason for this pattern is:

* Allow for two independent VMC contract instances which use different config values.
* Allow for environment configuration to be set at runtime *after* module loading has occurred.

This test doesn't verify that the `attrs` that are sent were accepted by Pulp. Try something like this:

```python
    def test_01_create_content_unit(self):
        """Create content unit."""
        attrs = _gen_content_unit_attrs(self.artifact)
        self.content_unit.update(self.client.post(FILE_CONTENT_PATH, attrs))
        for key, val in attrs.items():
            with self.subTest(key=key):
                self.assertEqual(self.content_unit[key], val)
```

Note that I call `self.content_unit.update()` instead of overwriting `type(self).content_unit`. Calling `update()` is safer, as it prevents certain classes of mistakes. Consider the following code:

```python
type(self).contennt_unit = self.client.post(FILE_CONTENT_PATH, attrs)
self.contennt_unit.update(self.client.post(FILE_CONTENT_PATH, attrs))
```

The former line of code will create a brand new attribute called `contennt_unit`, whereas the latter will throw an invalid attribute error.

There are a couple different exception mechanisms.

1. If a task raises an exception, then we essentially store a custom exception object in the object store. When a worker/driver retrieves that object, it checks the type and will raise an exception.
2. If some issue occurs in the backend or somewhere other than in a task, and we want to bring that issue to the attention of the driver, we have a mechanism for that. From C++ it is https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7edc677304f90bcab0a01e9722e59e36dd9afcb0/src/ray/gcs/tables.h#L470-L471 and from Python it is https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/7edc677304f90bcab0a01e9722e59e36dd9afcb0/python/ray/utils.py#L53-L57 (which uses the same C++ code path).

We'll need something similar to mechanism 1 for the gateway. That is
- We need a normal way to return values from the gateway to external client.
- For tasks that raise exceptions, this return value should just be a special exception object.
- When the external client gets a return value, it checks to see if the object is a special exception object and if so, it raises an exception.

What do you think about something like that?

We may need to be more careful about which functions (node vs. tail) should return a Redis reply and when, to make sure that every Redis command returns exactly one reply. I think it should be:
1. If node func errors, then it should return an error reply.
2. If node func does not error, then it should not return anything.
3. Tail func should always return a Redis reply.

I think this means that for the master commit on [credis](https://github.com/ray-project/credis/blob/master/src/chain_module.h#L352), we are incorrectly handling the case where the node func does not error and the command is executed on a non-tail node. In this case, the node func does not return a reply and the tail func isn't run, so we won't be returning a Redis reply.
